1.exposition: The seals begin to break, and with each of the first four a living creature cries “Come!” and a horse gallops onto the stage. The rhythm is fixed and formal — seal opened, creature's summons, horse and rider, a brief mandate — binding the four into one vision. A white horse whose crowned rider carries a bow rides out conquering, and to conquer; a red horse whose rider is given a great sword to take peace from the earth so that men kill one another; a black horse whose rider holds a balance while a voice prices out famine bread, yet spares the oil and the wine; and a pale horse whose rider is named Death, with Hades at his heels, given power over a fourth part of the earth to kill by sword, hunger, pestilence, and beasts. Conquest, war, famine, death — history's whole catalogue of terror, and not one horse rides but as the Lamb breaks a seal.
1.original_language: The four horses are named by colour, and each colour carries its freight: leukos (white, triumph and purity), pyrros (fiery red, the hue of blood and slaughter), melas (black, mourning and dearth), and — most vividly — chlōros, the yellow-green pallor of a corpse, the root behind “chlorine,” the colour of death itself. The first rider carries a toxon (“bow”) and goes forth nikōn kai hina nikēsē (“conquering, and that he might conquer”), the aorist reaching toward final, decisive victory. That first rider is the chapter's great crux: the same leukos and crown reappear on the Word of God in Revelation 19:11, which pulls one way; the coherence of the following three riders as calamities pulls the other.
1.historical_context: The imagery is not invented but inherited. The coloured horses ride out of Zechariah 6:2–8, the patrolling chariots of the four winds; the fourfold scourge of sword, famine, wild beasts, and pestilence is the covenant curse of Leviticus 26:26 and the “four sore judgments” of Ezekiel 14:21. The bow would strike an ancient reader as the weapon of the mounted Parthian archer, Rome's dreaded eastern foe. The black horse's economics are exact: a “penny” (denarius) was a day's wage and a “measure” of wheat a single day's ration, so a whole day's labour buys one man's bread — subsistence famine — while the oil and wine of the well-off go untouched.
1.christ: The christological anchor of the whole sequence is a single repeated clause: the Lamb opens the seals (vv.1, 3, 5, 7). The disasters do not escape his hand; they are loosed only as the one slain and found worthy (chapter 5) unrolls the scroll. Even the last rider is on a leash — Death and Hades ride, but their keys hang at the belt of the risen Christ (Revelation 1:18), and their commission runs no further than a fourth of the earth. History's terrors are not the triumph of chaos but the disclosure of purposes the Lamb alone can open.

verses.note: The Lamb opens the first seal and John hears, as it were the noise of thunder, a living creature cry “Come and see.” latin-fathers Victorinus marks the moment the sealed book at last begins to unfold — the Lamb who alone was worthy now breaks its first fastening. grammatical-historical Barnes identifies the summoning creature as the first of the four, the lion-like one, and reads “Come and see” as addressed to John, called to draw near and behold; grammatical-historical Robertson notes the same rhythmic formula will toll at the opening of each seal (vv.1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 12).

The crux of the chapter: a white horse, its rider crowned, bearing a bow, going forth “conquering, and to conquer.” The witnesses divide. latin-fathers Victorinus, reformed Gill, puritan Matthew Henry, and grammatical-historical Adam Clarke read the rider as Christ or his gospel — the word of preaching sent forth with the Spirit in the apostolic age, riding in triumph through the world; grammatical-historical Ellicott makes him the very symbol of Christian victory, though the bow already hints at judgments to come. But grammatical-historical Robertson resists tying this rider to the Christ of Revelation 19:11 — beyond the white horse, he says, the two have nothing in common — and wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads him flatly as Roman conquest, Trajan carrying the empire's bounds outward. The colour and crown all agree spell victory; whose victory is the debate.

At the second seal the second living creature says, Come and see. puritan Matthew Henry marks the turn: after the bright vision of the conquering gospel, the next three seals give “a sad prospect of great and desolating judgments” upon those who refuse or abuse it. grammatical-historical Barnes reads the four living creatures as emblems of God's providential rule, each in turn summoning the seer to the scene his order concerns.

Out rides the red horse, its rider given a great sword and power to take peace from the earth so that men “should kill one another.” reformed Gill weighs whether the red (the colour of blood) figures the suffering of the church or the bloody convulsions of the empire; either way the scene is mutual slaughter. puritan Matthew Henry names it the desolating judgment of war and adds the sovereign note: whether the rider be Christ as Lord of hosts or the instruments he raises up, the sword of war goes out only at his command — Christ rules in the kingdom of providence as of grace.

The third seal, the third creature, and a black horse whose rider holds a pair of balances. grammatical-historical Barnes reads the black (the colour of mourning) and the scales together as the sign of scarcity — food weighed out with anxious care. puritan Matthew Henry hears in the balance the threat of Leviticus 26:26, bread delivered again by weight, the staff of life dealt out in famine measure.

A voice from among the living creatures prices the dearth: a measure of wheat for a penny, three of barley for a penny — a day's wage for a day's bread — “and see thou hurt not the oil and the wine.” reformed Gill takes the voice for Christ's own, sounding from the midst of the throne, and marks the mercy folded into the judgment: grain is scarce and dear, yet the oil and wine are spared, so the famine, severe as it is, is bounded and not utter. The luxuries of the rich survive while the poor man's loaf is rationed — judgment measured, not total.

The fourth seal, and the last of the four creatures — the flying eagle — says, Come and see. grammatical-historical Barnes notes only the orderly continuance of the pattern: each creature in its turn has called the seer to look, and this fourth summons opens the way to the most terrible rider of all.

The pale horse: its rider named Death, with Hades following, given power over a fourth of the earth to kill by sword, hunger, pestilence, and wild beasts. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the ghastly precision of chlōros, the greenish pallor of a corpse; reformed Gill sees this rider gather up all the foregoing plagues — the sword of the red horse, the famine of the black — into one comprehensive judgment, the fourfold scourge of Ezekiel 14:21. Yet the commission is bounded to “the fourth part,” for even Death rides on the leash of the Lamb who holds his keys (Revelation 1:18).

witnesses.point: The first rider on the white horse is the word of preaching sent into the world with the Holy Spirit, conquering unbelief; the other three horses plainly signify the wars, famines, and pestilences the Lord foretold.
The white horse is the pure but despised gospel going forth conquering; the next three seals are God's desolating judgments — war, famine, death — upon those who refuse or abuse it.
Declines to identify the first rider with the Christ of 19:11 — beyond the white horse the two riders have nothing in common; the fourfold imagery echoes the horses of Zechariah.
Reads the seals as a historicist sequence — the gospel's apostolic triumph, then the sword, the famine, and the death that overtook the Roman world — each judgment bounded by God's permission.

external.name: Robert H. Mounce
external.point: Reads the first rider not as Christ but as a personification of militaristic conquest — the opening member of a unified series of four calamities — holding that its surface resemblance to the warrior of 19:11 should not override the difference.

1.application: The four horsemen name the very terrors we most dread — the conqueror, the sword, the empty cupboard, the grave. Revelation neither denies them nor tames them, but frames them: not one rides loose. Each waits on the hand of the slain Lamb, whose keys unlock even Death, and whose purposes the whole grim cavalcade unknowingly serves.

9.exposition: The fifth seal breaks the pattern: no horse, no rider, no summons — instead the scene lifts from earth to the sanctuary of heaven. John sees under the altar the souls of them that were slain for the word of God and their testimony, and hears them cry with a loud voice, How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood? The answer is a white robe and a word of patience: they are to rest yet for a little season, until the full number of their fellow-servants, still to be killed, is complete. The seals have moved from the instruments of earth's suffering to its victims — and the victims are not silenced but heard, robed, and told the day is fixed.
9.original_language: The martyrs are seen hypokatō tou thysiastēriou (“under the altar”), their psychai (“souls”) manifestly alive and conscious after death. They were esphagmenōn (“slaughtered”) — the same stark verb used of the Lamb “as it had been slain” in Revelation 5:6; their death is stamped with his. They died for their martyria (“testimony”), the very word from which “martyr” is carved. Their cry heōs pote (“how long”) is the ancient idiom of the lament psalms, and they address God as despotēs (“Sovereign Master,” the owner of a slave) — fitting on the lips of those whose Lord had let them die.
9.historical_context: The heavenly altar answers to the bronze altar of burnt offering in the temple, at whose base the blood of the sacrifice was poured out (Leviticus 4:7). To place the martyrs' souls “under the altar” is to name their deaths a sacrifice — their lifeblood poured at its foot. Their “how long?” is the very cadence of Israel's laments over shed blood (Psalms 79:5; Psalms 94:3), not private vengeance but a plea to the Judge of all the earth. And the “number yet to be fulfilled” assumes a divine tally of witnesses: persecution has a fixed limit, past which the end will come.
9.christ: The martyrs are described in the exact terms used of Christ and of John — “slain for the word of God, and for the testimony” — the phrase that marks Jesus the faithful witness (Revelation 1:5) and John his brother in tribulation (Revelation 1:9). Their sacrificial death under the altar is patterned on the Lamb's own; their waiting mirrors his — the slain Lamb who stands. The white robes given them are the overcomer's promised garment (Revelation 3:5), which becomes at last the bright linen of the bride (Revelation 19:8): vindication clothed on them now, consummated then.

verses.note: Under the altar John sees the souls of them that were slain for the word of God. reformed Gill calls this a dark time for church and ministry, and draws the doctrine the scene teaches: these are living, conscious souls after death — the soul does not perish or sleep with the body but survives to plead before God. latin-fathers Bede, reading the seals as a sequence, sees here “the glory of those who triumph” in the threefold war of the three preceding riders. That the verb for “slain” is the Lamb's own (Revelation 5:6) binds their martyrdom to his sacrifice.

They cry with a loud voice, How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood? reformed Gill notes the ardour and importunity of the cry — further proof they are awake and not asleep — and insists it is no private vindictiveness but an appeal to God as holy (whose holiness must punish sin) and true (whose truth must keep his word). It is the lament of Psalms 79:5 lifted to the throne: the suffering church longing for the day of God's righteous vindication.

Each is given a white robe and bidden rest “yet for a little season,” until the number of their fellow-martyrs is complete. reformed Gill reads the robe as a token of their innocence — they suffered for no crime but were righteous in God's sight — and their present blessedness. grammatical-historical Barnes adds that the vengeance is deferred, not denied: the robes are the pledge of divine favour and certain triumph, the delay a measured patience while the full company of witnesses is gathered. Not indifference, but a fixed and merciful reckoning.

witnesses.point: The souls under the altar are the martyrs, alive and conscious after death; their fervent cry proves the soul does not sleep with the body, and their white robes are the token of their innocence.

The white robes are emblems of purity, triumph, and the divine favour; the answer to the martyrs is that vengeance will not be immediate but is certain — they are to wait until the number of the slain is filled up.
In the unfolding of the seals the fifth shows the glory of those who triumph in the threefold war waged against the church in the three preceding seals.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: Hears in the martyrs' 'how long' and the command to wait until the number is complete the book's conviction that God's kingdom advances through the faithful witness of those who share the Lamb's suffering; the delay is not divine indifference but the gathering of the full company of witnesses.

9.application: “How long?” is one of Scripture's most honest prayers — grief and faith in a single breath. It does not suppress the outrage of injustice but carries it straight to the throne, and there it is answered: not “never,” and not yet “now,” but “rest — the day is fixed, the number known, the robe already yours.” Faithful lament is not the failure of trust but its costliest form.

12.exposition: The sixth seal opens onto cosmic collapse. A great earthquake shakes the frame of things; the sun turns black as sackcloth, the moon to blood; the stars of heaven fall like unripe figs in a gale; the sky is rolled up as a scroll, and every mountain and island is wrenched from its place. Then the response: every rank of humanity, from kings to slaves, hides in caves and begs the rocks, Fall on us, and hide us from the face of him that sitteth on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb — for the great day of his wrath is come; and who shall be able to stand? The question is left hanging in the air; the book will not answer it until the sealing and the great multitude of the next chapter.
12.original_language: Every image here is quarried from the Old Testament's “day of the LORD.” The blackened sun and blood-red moon come from Joel 2:10 and Joel 2:31; the stars dropping like figs and the heaven rolled together as a scroll are the judgment of Isaiah 34:4; the flight into caves “for fear of the LORD” is Isaiah 2:19; the cry to the mountains, “Fall on us,” is Hosea 10:8, the very words Jesus set on the lips of the last days (Luke 23:30). At the centre stands the book's sharpest oxymoron: hē orgē tou arniou — “the wrath of the Lamb.” The gentlest of creatures, the slain Lamb, is now the terror from whom the mighty flee.
12.historical_context: Here, as reformed Gill admits, “interpreters much differ,” and the four great schools part ways. Preterists read the convulsion as the shaking of the old Jewish and Roman order in the first century; historicists like Gill and Barnes refer it to the overthrow of the pagan Roman empire under Constantine, the “earthquake” figuring the collapse of thrones and the darkened lights the fall of rulers; futurists take the cosmic signs as the literal upheavals of the last day; idealists see the recurring pattern of God's judgment shaking every proud power to its foundations. Barnes states the prophetic idiom plainly: earthquakes in this language are “symbols of violent and important changes,” thrones overturned and an old order passing away.
12.christ: The heart of the scene is a phrase that ought to be a contradiction: “the wrath of the Lamb.” The Lamb slain to ransom the nations (chapter 5) is the one whose face the powerful cannot bear. This is no contradiction but a unity: the same cross that grounds salvation measures the judgment on those who refuse it. And the wrath is shared — it belongs to “him that sitteth on the throne, and… the Lamb” (v.16) alike. Christ is not the merciful advocate shielding sinners from an angry Father; he is co-judge with the Father, the rejected redemption itself become the ground of the great day.

verses.note: The sixth seal brings a great earthquake, the sun black as sackcloth of hair, the moon as blood. reformed Gill concedes interpreters much differ — some refer it to Jerusalem's fall — but applies it most largely to the overthrow of the heathen Roman empire under Constantine. grammatical-historical Barnes reads the earthquake as the stock prophetic symbol of political revolution, dynasties overturned and a new order springing up; the darkened sun and moon are the language of Joel 2:31.

The stars of heaven fell unto the earth, like a fig tree casting her untimely figs in a mighty wind. reformed Gill, in the historicist reading, takes the stars for the high and exalted — rulers, dignitaries, the inferior deities of paganism — cast down from their dignity. The vivid figure is lifted straight from Isaiah 34:4: when God shakes the heavens, nothing lofty keeps its place.

The heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together, and every mountain and island was moved out of its place. reformed Gill notes the ancient book was a rolled volume, and so the very heaven is rolled shut and put away — the completeness of the overturning, for even the most fixed and immovable things are displaced. The image again is Isaiah 34:4; and there is a quiet irony in a scroll-vision describing the sky rolled up like a scroll — the book unrolling the very end it depicts.

Then the flight: the kings of the earth, and the great men, and the rich men, and the chief captains, and the mighty men, and every bondman, and every free man, hiding in the dens and rocks. grammatical-historical Barnes says the design is to depict the universal consternation, the dismay seizing every class; reformed Gill glosses the kings as the Roman emperors and magistrates. The six-fold roll from throne to slave leaves no rank exempt — all alike scramble for the caves of Isaiah 2:19.

They cry to the mountains and rocks, Fall on us, and hide us… from the wrath of the Lamb. reformed Gill marks the depth of the despair: they choose death under crushing stone over the face of the Judge. The words are Hosea's, “say to the mountains, Cover us” (Hosea 10:8), which Christ himself foretold (Luke 23:30). And the terror is the “wrath of the Lamb” — the meek One's anger the more dreadful for the patience and grace so long despised: those who would not bow to his grace now flee his wrath.

For the great day of his wrath is come; and who shall be able to stand? reformed Gill sets it as the answer to the martyrs' “how long” (v.10): the day of God's and the Lamb's anger has arrived, and none can stand but those in Christ, covered with his righteousness. grammatical-historical Barnes hears in the question a cry of utter hopelessness — the once all-powerful persecutors confessing that no rank or power can survive that day. The question is real, and the book withholds its answer: it belongs to the sealed of the next chapter (Revelation 7:1–17).

witnesses.point: Grants that interpreters much differ, and applies the sixth seal most largely to the shaking and overthrow of the pagan Roman empire under Constantine — the earthquake and darkened lights figuring the fall of rulers; none can stand but those covered in Christ's righteousness.

Earthquakes in prophetic language are symbols of violent political revolution — thrones overturned, dynasties passing, a new order arising; the sixth seal depicts the universal consternation that seizes every class of men.
In the sixth seal are shown the things to come in the time of Antichrist, gathered up with a brief recapitulation of what has gone before.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: Reads the sixth seal's cosmic dissolution as the Old Testament 'day of the LORD' language — a figurative portrayal of final judgment and the undoing of the old creation rather than a coded reference to one empire's fall — and finds in 'the wrath of the Lamb' the paradox that the crucified one's self-giving becomes the ground of judgment on those who reject it.

12.application: The most terrifying words in the chapter are “the wrath of the Lamb,” because the Lamb is the last one the world expects to fear. Yet the same Lamb whose wrath the mighty flee is the only shelter from it. The unanswerable question — “who shall be able to stand?” — is not left unanswered forever: turn the page, and there stand the sealed, the great multitude washed in his blood. The rocks cannot hide you from the Lamb; only the Lamb can.

_meta.title: The Lamb Opens the Seals
reflection: The four horsemen ride out only as the Lamb breaks each seal (v.1). What changes in how you carry fear of the future when history's worst terrors are on the Lamb's leash, not loose?

The martyrs cry “How long, O Lord, holy and true?” and are told to rest a little longer (vv.10–11). Where do you need to bring an honest lament to God rather than either suppressing it or letting it curdle into bitterness?

The powerful flee “the wrath of the Lamb,” asking “who shall be able to stand?” (vv.16–17). The next chapter answers with those washed in his blood (Revelation 7:14). On which side of that question is your hope resting today?
